role_summary: Civil rights attorney with the Lawyers Constitutional Defense Committee (LCDC) in Louisiana 1965-1972; counsel for Gary Duncan in Duncan v. Louisiana, 391 U.S. 145, which Sobol argued at the United States Supreme Court in January 1968 and which held that the Fourteenth Amendment Due Process Clause incorporates the Sixth Amendment right to a jury trial against the states; arrested in February 1967 in Plaquemines Parish, Louisiana on charges of practicing law without a license, the case at the heart of the 2020 documentary A Crime on the Bayou.
ai_reading: Ranked by how many of Sobol's passages a neighbor recurs across, the joint John and Jean Rosenberg interview (entry 61) is his single largest neighbor at a count of 318, ahead of Robert L. Carter (entry 108, 274) and the two Bogalusa-anchored Louisiana interviews that actually score his highest individual passage similarities: Cynthia and Fletcher Anderson (entry 25, top passage 0.78) and the Bennett-Breaux-Jenkins Bogalusa interview (entry 46, top passage 0.78). The Rosenberg pairing is the structural surprise. The cultural record would file Sobol with his own Louisiana cooperating-attorney circle (Collins, Douglas, and Elie) or with the LDF litigators; the embedding instead pulls hardest toward John Rosenberg, who was not an LCDC volunteer at all but a U.S. Department of Justice Civil Rights Division attorney in the Mississippi section under John Doar before he ran the Appalachian Research and Defense Fund in eastern Kentucky for decades. What the cosine space seems to be catching is the federal civil-rights-enforcement register the two men share rather than any organizational tie, and Sobol's own testimony bears that out: the DOJ Civil Rights Division referred Gary Duncan to him, DOJ attorneys (Robert Moore and David Rose) shaped his Title VII seniority theory, and the DOJ intervened on his side in the case that bears his name. On the Sacred Framing versus Secular Framing axis the embedding places him at -0.76 (far toward Secular / Political Framing), the most extreme of his five axis readings, and the placement tracks the record: his work was institutionally secular (constitutional litigation, federal civil rights enforcement), and his own testimony stays in that legal register rather than the moral-vocabulary and church-adjacent organizing language of the Bogalusa testimony around him. The reading does not pick up the religious idiom of his immediate neighborhood, the Bogalusa Civic and Voters League and the church-network framing that runs through the Anderson (entry 25) and Bennett-Breaux-Jenkins (entry 46) interviews; the cosine space holds him to the secular-legal side instead.

interview_snippets.speaker: Richard Barry Sobol
interview_snippets.lead_in: Reflecting on his first three-week LCDC assignment in 1965, Sobol explains the conviction that led him to move to New Orleans full-time the next year:
interview_snippets.quote: And that I came away with the firm idea that what they need is permanent lawyers down there, and not people passing through.

interview_snippets.speaker: Richard Barry Sobol
interview_snippets.lead_in: Sobol recounts the roadside gesture by his client Gary Duncan that led to the battery charge at the center of Duncan v. Louisiana:
interview_snippets.quote: He touched one of the boys on the elbow and said, go ahead on.

interview_snippets.speaker: Richard Barry Sobol
interview_snippets.lead_in: After securing a stay of Duncan's sentence at the Plaquemines Parish courthouse in February 1967, Sobol describes his own arrest:
interview_snippets.quote: as I walked out, Leander Perez and a sheriff standing there in the hall arrested me.

interview_snippets.speaker: Richard Barry Sobol
interview_snippets.lead_in: On why the same civil rights claims succeeded before some federal judges and failed before others, Sobol returns to a theme he repeats across the interview:
interview_snippets.quote: It's all how the judge feels. It's all law falls away when you get down to those kind of decisions.

interview_snippets.speaker: Toni Breaux
interview_snippets.lead_in: In the Bogalusa group interview, Toni Breaux recalls the lawyers who worked with the Bogalusa Civic and Voters League and names "Dick Sobol" among them:
interview_snippets.quote: He was at the house all the time. You know, they would - like, he was - they would be meeting, and he would be counseling them and telling them what they needed to do and stuff.

biographical_paragraph: Richard Barry Sobol's Civil Rights History Project oral history is cross-referenced against the Library of Congress published transcript. His external biographical record is well-documented [src: 1, src: 2]. Sobol was born May 29, 1937 in New York City. He grew up on the West Side of Manhattan, graduated from the Bronx High School of Science in 1954, took a Bachelor's at Union College in Schenectady (1958) and a JD from Columbia Law School in 1961, where he ranked third in his class and served as Notes and Comments Editor of the Law Review [src: 2]. He clerked for Judge Paul Hays on the Second Circuit and for FTC Commissioner Philip Elman, then joined Arnold, Fortas & Porter in Washington, D.C. In summer 1965 he volunteered with the Lawyers Constitutional Defense Committee (LCDC) in Louisiana; what was intended as a three-week assignment became a fifty-year civil rights legal practice [src: 2]. In summer 1966 he took a leave from the firm, moved his family to New Orleans, and worked full-time for the LCDC for three years across Louisiana federal and state courts on school-desegregation, voter-registration, and civil rights-protester defense cases. He represented Gary Duncan in Duncan v. Louisiana, arguing the case at the Supreme Court on January 17, 1968; the May 20, 1968 decision (391 U.S. 145, 7-2) held that the Fourteenth Amendment Due Process Clause incorporates the Sixth Amendment right to a jury trial against the states, requiring jury trials in serious state criminal cases [src: 2]. In February 1967, while still representing Duncan, Sobol was arrested in Plaquemines Parish on charges of practicing law without a license, an arrest directed by arch-segregationist parish boss Leander Perez; the LCDC's federal action against Perez (the Sobol v. Perez case, 1968) became a landmark in cross-state-bar civil rights legal practice [src: 2]. Sobol continued civil rights and consumer-protection litigation through the next five decades, including the Dalkon Shield bankruptcy case for which his 1991 book won the 1992 American Bar Association Silver Gavel Award. He died March 24, 2020 at eighty-two [src: 2].
movement_context: The Lawyers Constitutional Defense Committee (LCDC) was founded in June 1964 by Henry Schwarzschild as an ACLU-affiliated emergency legal-defense apparatus assembled for the Mississippi Freedom Summer voter-registration campaign, drawing volunteer attorneys from the ACLU, the American Jewish Congress, the Congress of Racial Equality, the NAACP, and the National Council of Churches into a single Jackson-based coordinating office; the LCDC formally separated from the constituent organizations in 1965 and continued through the late 1960s as the principal volunteer-attorney pipeline for southern civil rights criminal-defense work [src: 3]. The LCDC's institutional distinction from the NAACP Legal Defense and Educational Fund (LDF) is that the LDF concentrated on civil rights affirmative litigation (school desegregation, voting rights, employment discrimination, public-accommodations enforcement), while the LCDC concentrated on the criminal defense of civil rights demonstrators, organizers, and field workers facing state-court prosecution for the direct-action campaigns themselves; the two organizations were complementary and often coordinated counsel on the same underlying organizing situations [src: 3]. The Bogalusa-Plaquemines federal litigation in which Sobol became the named plaintiff was the doctrinal apex of the LCDC's cross-state-bar legal-practice posture. After Sobol's February 1967 arrest in Plaquemines Parish on a state criminal complaint for practicing law without a Louisiana license, the LCDC filed a federal civil rights action in the Eastern District of Louisiana seeking to enjoin Leander Perez's prosecution; the three-judge federal district court in Sobol v. Perez, 289 F. Supp. 392 (E.D. La. 1968), held that the Louisiana practice-of-law statute as applied to out-of-state civil rights cooperating attorneys violated the Civil Rights Act of 1964 and the First and Fourteenth Amendments, and enjoined the prosecution; the ruling became the controlling federal precedent on cross-state-bar civil rights legal practice and the basis for later state-bar pro hac vice reform in the southern federal districts [src: 4]. The clientele Sobol defended during the LCDC Louisiana years were the same Bogalusa-anchored organizers documented in the Andersons (entry 25) and Bennett-Breaux-Jenkins (entry 46) catalog pages: members of the Bogalusa Civic and Voters League, the CORE Bogalusa field staff, and the Deacons of Defense and Justice chapter Fletcher Anderson helped found, plus the parallel Plaquemines Parish school-desegregation and voter-registration plaintiffs whose cases Leander Perez's parish apparatus had been suppressing for two decades [src: 5].
legacy_and_reception: After the LCDC years in Louisiana wound down in 1972, Sobol returned to private practice in Washington, D.C. but continued civil rights and public-interest litigation as a substantial fraction of his caseload through the next four decades [src: 2]. He represented claimants and represented the court-appointed claimants committee in the A. H. Robins Company / Dalkon Shield bankruptcy case in the 1980s, a mass-tort proceeding involving roughly two hundred thousand women injured by the contraceptive device; his 1991 University of Chicago Press book Bending the Law: The Story of the Dalkon Shield Bankruptcy won the 1992 American Bar Association Silver Gavel Award for legal writing [src: 2]. The 2020 documentary feature A Crime on the Bayou, directed by Nancy Buirski and released the year of Sobol's death, reconstructed the Duncan v. Louisiana and Sobol v. Perez record from contemporaneous interviews with Sobol and Gary Duncan and brought the Plaquemines Parish prosecution back into public attention; the film treated the Sobol-Duncan attorney-client relationship as the through-line of a single decade-long litigation arc rather than two separate cases [src: 6]. Sobol died March 24, 2020 at his home in Sebastopol, California, at the age of eighty-two [src: 9]; the obituary in the New York Times described the Plaquemines arrest, the Supreme Court argument in Duncan, and the federal injunction against Perez as the central legal achievements of his career [src: 7].

gallery.alt: Leander Henry Perez, 1914 Tulane University yearbook portrait
gallery.caption: Leander Henry Perez (1891-1969), portrait photograph from the 1914 Jambalaya, the Tulane University yearbook, page 105. Perez was the Plaquemines Parish district attorney and parish boss who directed Sobol's February 1967 arrest on charges of practicing law without a Louisiana license; the resulting federal action, Sobol v. Perez, 289 F. Supp. 392 (E.D. La. 1968), enjoined the prosecution and became the controlling federal precedent on cross-state-bar civil rights legal practice.
gallery.repository: Wikimedia Commons (sourced from the 1914 Tulane Jambalaya yearbook)
gallery.license: Public domain (U.S. publication prior to January 1, 1931)

gallery.alt: Plaquemines Parish Courthouse, Pointe a la Hache, Louisiana, September 2009
gallery.caption: The upriver corner of the Plaquemines Parish Courthouse in Pointe a la Hache, Louisiana, photographed September 14, 2009 by Infrogmation of New Orleans. The Plaquemines Parish courthouse complex was the venue of the February 1967 criminal proceedings against Sobol on charges of practicing law without a Louisiana license, the proceedings the LCDC enjoined in federal court in Sobol v. Perez.

gallery.alt: Promised Land, the Leander Perez house on Highway 39, Plaquemines Parish, Louisiana, 2012
gallery.caption: Promised Land, the Plaquemines Parish residence of Leander Perez on Highway 39, photographed March 10, 2012 by Infrogmation of New Orleans. Perez's three-decade Plaquemines Parish boss rule from the late 1930s through the late 1960s was the institutional context for the February 1967 arrest of Sobol; the federal injunction in Sobol v. Perez was one of the first sustained federal-court interventions against the Perez parish apparatus.
